explicit_text: I first learned about the attacks on the World Trade Center while I was in a meeting with York County discussing land exchanges. I got a call from Greg Stiles in Shenandoah just 15 or 20 minutes after the first plane hit. It was a shocking moment, and we immediately prepared the Type-1 Team to be on alert for deployment. The incident marked the beginning of a major response effort that involved coordination across many teams and locations.
implicit_text: I first learned about the attacks on the twin towers in New York City while I was in a meeting with York County discussing land exchanges. I got a call from Greg Stiles in Shenandoah just 15 or 20 minutes after the first plane hit. It was a shocking moment, and we immediately prepared the Type-1 Team to be on alert for deployment. The incident marked the beginning of a major response effort that involved coordination across many teams and locations.

explicit_text: I was in York County when I first heard about the World Trade Center attacks. We were discussing land exchanges when I received the call about the plane hitting the Trade Center. York County was where I was physically present at that moment, and it was the starting point before I mobilized to Shenandoah with the team. It was an unexpected place to get such critical news.
implicit_text: I was in the county north of Virginia Beach when I first heard about the attacks on the twin towers in New York City. We were discussing land exchanges when I received the call about the plane hitting the towers. That county was where I was physically present at that moment, and it was the starting point before I mobilized to Shenandoah with the team. It was an unexpected place to get such critical news.

explicit_text: Shenandoah National Park played a crucial role during the response to the World Trade Center attacks. Greg Stiles, who works in Shenandoah, was the one who alerted me to prepare the Type-1 Team. We set up our operations there and stayed in Luray, which is nearby. Shenandoah was also the main dispatch system for communications, and a Type-2 Team was stationed there as a backup to support the National Parks during the crisis.
implicit_text: The national park along the Blue Ridge Mountains played a crucial role during the response to the attacks on the twin towers. Greg Stiles, who works in that park, was the one who alerted me to prepare the Type-1 Team. We set up our operations there and stayed in Luray, which is nearby. That park was also the main dispatch system for communications, and a Type-2 Team was stationed there as a backup to support the National Parks during the crisis.

explicit_text: Luray, Virginia, was where we stayed while operating out of Shenandoah National Park during the World Trade Center incident. Dennis McGinnis and I were among the first responders to arrive in that area. It served as a base for our team as we coordinated the response and managed communications. Being close to Shenandoah was strategic for our operations.
implicit_text: The small town in Virginia’s Shenandoah Valley was where we stayed while operating out of the nearby national park during the twin towers incident. Dennis McGinnis and I were among the first responders to arrive in that area. It served as a base for our team as we coordinated the response and managed communications. Being close to the national park was strategic for our operations.

explicit_text: Dennis McGinnis, who was my Operations Section Chief during the World Trade Center response, had previously been at Cape Hatteras National Seashore. I remember this because he drove up from that area to join me. His experience and proximity helped us get organized quickly at Shenandoah. Cape Hatteras was part of the broader network of National Park Service locations involved in the response.
implicit_text: Dennis McGinnis, who was my Operations Section Chief during the twin towers response, had previously been at the national seashore on the Outer Banks of North Carolina. I remember this because he drove up from that area to join me. His experience and proximity helped us get organized quickly at Shenandoah. That seashore was part of the broader network of National Park Service locations involved in the response.

explicit_text: The Incident Command System (ICS) was the backbone of our response to the World Trade Center attacks. Our Type-1 Team was structured around ICS functions like Incident Commander, Planning, Operations, Finance, Logistics, and Information Officer. Although the system works well, we faced challenges because some team members couldn't arrive quickly due to travel restrictions. We had to adapt and eventually brought in additional personnel to maintain effective command and control.

explicit_text: J.D. Swed, who was from Indiana Dunes National Park, came with us as a deputy Incident Commander because he could drive in when flights were limited. His ability to travel by car was invaluable since many team members were delayed. Indiana Dunes thus contributed personnel to the response effort, helping us maintain leadership continuity during a difficult time.

explicit_text: The September 11 attacks were a defining moment in my career. I first heard about the planes hitting the World Trade Center while in York County, and immediately we mobilized the Type-1 Team. The incident required rapid coordination across multiple National Park Service locations and the Department of the Interior. It was a complex, prolonged response that tested our preparedness and ability to support both the parks and the Department.
implicit_text: Those attacks on the twin towers in New York City were a defining moment in my career. I first heard about the planes hitting the towers while in York County, and immediately we mobilized the Type-1 Team. The incident required rapid coordination across multiple National Park Service locations and the Department of the Interior. It was a complex, prolonged response that tested our preparedness and ability to support both the parks and the Department.

explicit_text: The National Park Service was central to the response during the September 11 attacks. Our Type-1 Team was activated under its authority, and we worked closely with the Director who delegated authority to us. The Service's continuity of operations plans were in place but somewhat hampered because many plans were packed away during a move. Despite that, many Rangers and staff knew exactly what to do and implemented the all-risk management plan effectively.
implicit_text: The federal agency responsible for managing national parks was central to the response during the attacks on the twin towers. Our Type-1 Team was activated under its authority, and we worked closely with the Director who delegated authority to us. The agency's continuity of operations plans were in place but somewhat hampered because many plans were packed away during a move. Despite that, many Rangers and staff knew exactly what to do and implemented the all-risk management plan effectively.

explicit_text: The Department of the Interior played a significant role during the September 11 response, and our team was tasked with supporting both the National Park Service and the Department. This was the first time I encountered a delegation of authority that explicitly included supporting the Department's needs. We had to balance resource allocation carefully, especially law enforcement rangers, to meet both Department and park requirements. The Department was less familiar with the Incident Command System, so we helped them develop their own ICS structure.
implicit_text: The federal department overseeing natural resources and public lands played a significant role during the attacks response, and our team was tasked with supporting both the national parks agency and this department. This was the first time I encountered a delegation of authority that explicitly included supporting the department's needs. We had to balance resource allocation carefully, especially law enforcement rangers, to meet both department and park requirements. The department was less familiar with the Incident Command System, so we helped them develop their own ICS structure.

explicit_text: I was involved in the Y2K preparations with the National Park Service and the Department of the Interior before the September 11 attacks. Y2K was more of a process to safeguard systems rather than an actual incident. However, it gave my team experience working with the Department and supporting their needs, which helped during the more complex response to the 9/11 attacks. The delegation of authority during Y2K came from Deny Galvin, and it was less involved than what we saw later.
implicit_text: I was involved in the millennium bug preparations with the national parks agency and the federal department before the attacks on the twin towers. That event was more of a process to safeguard systems rather than an actual incident. However, it gave my team experience working with the department and supporting their needs, which helped during the more complex response to the 2001 attacks. The delegation of authority during that time came from Deny Galvin, and it was less involved than what we saw later.

explicit_text: During the September 11 response, the Bureau of Reclamation was one of the Department of the Interior agencies we supported. They had a particular need for law enforcement rangers, which were in high demand. Balancing their requests with the needs of the National Parks was challenging, but we worked closely with the Director and Rick Gale to allocate resources effectively. Supporting the Bureau was part of our broader mission to assist the Department during this major incident.

explicit_text: Gary Allen was involved in discussions about creating a unified command system during the September 11 response. We had meetings with him to explore how to better coordinate between the National Park Service and the Department of the Interior. His input was part of our efforts to improve communication and resource management during the incident. Working with Gary helped us understand the challenges of integrating different agencies under one command structure.

explicit_text: Rick Gale served as my advisor for the Director during the September 11 incident. He played a critical role in helping balance resource requests, especially when it came to allocating law enforcement rangers between the Department and the National Parks. Rick was involved in making final decisions alongside the Director to ensure both sets of needs were met. His guidance was invaluable in navigating the complexities of this major incident.
